name: Constructed-Response Realism Requires Plausible Imperfection and Cognitive-Level Variation
statement: Wu et al. provide constructed-response evidence that realism depends on simulating plausible mistakes and cognitive-level variation, because baseline helpful-assistant-style LLMs tend to overproduce advanced or correct responses.
population_or_context: Python programming simulation with the Student_100 dataset.
quantitative_note: The paper reports a Student_100 dataset of 100 students and 5,000 learning records and claims 100% improvement in simulation accuracy and realism over baseline models.

source_locator.section: Abstract; Experiments; In-Depth Analysis; Conclusion; Limitations; Ethics Statement
source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase_note: Extraction uses the abstract's problem framing and dataset, the component-ablation discussion, and the controlled-setting limitations.

caveats: The study is not an automated scoring validation study.
The domain is programming and may not transfer directly to essay or other constructed-response assessments.
The authors explicitly characterize the work as early-stage controlled experimentation.
evaluation_method: Simulation accuracy/realism metrics, ablations, difficulty-by-student analysis, and human evaluation.
limitations: No operational assessment or scorer-validation context.
